question_html: A student is asked to connect four cells, each of emf E and internal resistance r, in series. But she/he connects one cell wrongly in series with the other cells. The equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination will be
question_text: A student is asked to connect four cells, each of emf E and internal resistance r, in series. But she/he connects one cell wrongly in series with the other cells. The equivalent emf and the equivalent internal resistance of the combination will be

options.explanation: When four cells, each with an emf {tex}E{/tex} and internal resistance {tex}r{/tex} , are connected in series but one cell is connected in the reverse direction, the situation can be analyzed as follows: 1. Equivalent EMF: Three cells contribute positively: {tex}E+E+E=3 E{/tex} One cell contributes negatively: {tex}-E{/tex} Thus, the total equivalent emf will be: {tex} E_{\text {total }}=3 E-E=2 E{/tex} 2. Equivalent Internal Resistance: Since all cells are in series, the total internal resistance is simply the sum of the internal resistances: {tex} r_{\text {total }}=r+r+r+r=4 r{/tex} Therefore, the equivalent emf is {tex}2 E{/tex} and the equivalent internal resistance is {tex}4 r{/tex} .

explanation: When four cells, each with an emf {tex}E{/tex} and internal resistance {tex}r{/tex} , are connected in series but one cell is connected in the reverse direction, the situation can be analyzed as follows: 1. Equivalent EMF: Three cells contribute positively: {tex}E+E+E=3 E{/tex} One cell contributes negatively: {tex}-E{/tex} Thus, the total equivalent emf will be: {tex} E_{\text {total }}=3 E-E=2 E{/tex} 2. Equivalent Internal Resistance: Since all cells are in series, the total internal resistance is simply the sum of the internal resistances: {tex} r_{\text {total }}=r+r+r+r=4 r{/tex} Therefore, the equivalent emf is {tex}2 E{/tex} and the equivalent internal resistance is {tex}4 r{/tex} .

question_html: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use these rules to write the expressions for the currents I1, I2 and I3 in the circuit diagram shown in figure below.
question_text: State Kirchhoff's rules. Use these rules to write the expressions for the currents I 1 , I 2 and I 3 in the circuit diagram shown in figure below.
explanation: Kirchhoff's first rule: The algebraic sum of the electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero, i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma I = 0{/tex} Kirchhoffs second rule or loop rule In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero. i.e. {tex}\Sigma E + \Sigma I R = 0{/tex} For given circuit, At, F, applying junction rule, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In mesh ABCFA, -2 - 4I 1 + 3I 2 + 1 = 0 4I 1 - 3I 2 = -1 ...(ii) In mesh FCDEF, -1 - 3I 2 - 2I 3 + 4 = 0 3I 2 + 2I 3 = 3 On solving, I 1 , I 2 and I 3 , we get {tex}I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { 13 } A , \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { 7 } { 13 } A{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 } A{/tex} Thus by using KCL & KVL, we can calculate the values of current flowing in each branch.
solution: Kirchhoff's first rule: The algebraic sum of the electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero, i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction. {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma I = 0{/tex} Kirchhoffs second rule or loop rule In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero. i.e. {tex}\Sigma E + \Sigma I R = 0{/tex} For given circuit, At, F, applying junction rule, I 3 = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) In mesh ABCFA, -2 - 4I 1 + 3I 2 + 1 = 0 4I 1 - 3I 2 = -1 ...(ii) In mesh FCDEF, -1 - 3I 2 - 2I 3 + 4 = 0 3I 2 + 2I 3 = 3 On solving, I 1 , I 2 and I 3 , we get {tex}I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { 13 } A , \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { 7 } { 13 } A{/tex} and {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 } A{/tex} Thus by using KCL & KVL, we can calculate the values of current flowing in each branch.
solution_html:
Kirchhoff's first rule: The algebraic sum of the electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero, i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction.
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \Sigma I = 0{/tex}
Kirchhoffs second rule or loop rule In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emfs of cells and the product of currents and resistances is always equal to zero.
i.e. {tex}\Sigma E + \Sigma I R = 0{/tex}

For given circuit,

At, F, applying junction rule,
I3 = I1 + I2 ...(i)
In mesh ABCFA,
-2 - 4I1 + 3I2 + 1 = 0
4I1 - 3I2 = -1 ...(ii)
In mesh FCDEF,
-1 - 3I2 - 2I3 + 4 = 0
3I2 + 2I3 = 3
On solving, I1, I2 and I3, we get
{tex}I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 } { 13 } A , \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { 7 } { 13 } A{/tex}
and {tex}I _ { 3 } = \frac { 9 } { 13 } A{/tex}
Thus by using KCL & KVL, we can calculate the values of current flowing in each branch.

question_text: Use Kirchhoff’s rules to obtain the balance condition in Wheatstone bridge. Give one practical application that is based on this principle.
explanation: Applying kirchoff’s loop rule to ADBA and CBDC -I 1 R 1 + 0 + I 2 R 2 = 0 I 2 R 4 + 0 - I 1 R 3 = 0 Since, I g = 0, I 3 = I 1 , I 4 = I 2 {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_4}{R_3}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} (Balance Condition) A practical device using the principle of wheatstone bridge is meter bridge.
solution: Applying kirchoff’s loop rule to ADBA and CBDC -I 1 R 1 + 0 + I 2 R 2 = 0 I 2 R 4 + 0 - I 1 R 3 = 0 Since, I g = 0, I 3 = I 1 , I 4 = I 2 {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_4}{R_3} \quad{/tex} and {tex}\frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{R_4}{R_3}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}{/tex} (Balance Condition) A practical device using the principle of wheatstone bridge is meter bridge.

    -I1R1 + 0 + I2R2 = 0

explanation: Suppose PQ is a conductor of length l and a potential difference V is applied across PQ as shown in the figure. i.e., its P end remains negative and Q end positive. Therefore an electric field is developed directing from Q to P and its intensity is given by E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(i) Each free electron of the conductor experiences a force (F) towards the end Q, {tex}\therefore{/tex} F = -eE If m be the mass of the electron, then its acceleration = {tex}\frac{\vec{F}}{m}{/tex} or {tex}\vec{a}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} The average thermal velocity of electrons is zero. {tex}\therefore{/tex} u = 0 Therefore using first equation of motion (v = u + at), we have {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = 0 + {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex} or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\left(\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m}\right) \tau{/tex} or {tex}\overrightarrow{v_d}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-e \vec{E}}{m} \tau{/tex} ...(ii) Numerically we can write, {tex}\left|\overrightarrow{v_d}\right|{/tex} = {tex}\frac{e E}{m} \tau{/tex} {tex}\because{/tex} From equation (i), E = {tex}\frac{V}{l}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} v d = {tex}\frac{e \tau}{m} \frac{V}{l}{/tex} ...(iii)

question_html: For the circuit diagram of a Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure, use Kirchhoff's laws to obtain its balance condition.
question_text: For the circuit diagram of a Wheatstone bridge shown in the figure, use Kirchhoff's laws to obtain its balance condition.
explanation: In balanced Wheatstone bridge if no current flow through galvanometer, that means while applying Kirchhoff's law, we can neglect this path. No current flows through the galvanometer G when circuit is balanced. Applying Kirchhoff's second rule, we have In mesh ABDA, {tex}\therefore{/tex} -I 1 R 1 + (I - I 1 ) R 4 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R 1 = (I - I 1 ) R 4 .... (i) In mesh BCDB, -I 1 R 2 + (I - I 1 ) R 3 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R 2 = (I - I 1 ) R 3 ...(ii) On dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\frac { I _ { 1 } R _ { 1 } } { I _ { 1 } R _ { 2 } } = \frac { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 4 } } { \left( I - I _ { 1 } \right) R _ { 3 } }\Rightarrow\; \frac { R _ { 1 } } { R _ { 2 } } = \frac { R _ { 4 } } { R _ { 3 } }{/tex} This is necessary and required balanced condition of balanced Wheatstone bridge.
solution: In balanced Wheatstone bridge if no current flow through galvanometer, that means while applying Kirchhoff's law, we can neglect this path. question_html: Draw a plot showing the variation of resistivity of a (i) conductor and (ii) semiconductor, with the increase in temperature. How does one explain this behaviour in terms of number density of charge carriers and the relaxation time?
question_text: Draw a plot showing the variation of resistivity of a (i) conductor and (ii) semiconductor, with the increase in temperature. How does one explain this behaviour in terms of number density of charge carriers and the relaxation time?
explanation: For conductor For semiconductor Relaxation time is the time between two successive collisions of an electron with atoms/ions of the conductor. The relation between resistivity and relaxation time is given by: {tex}\rho = \frac { m } { n e ^ { 2 } \tau }{/tex} In conductors, average relaxation time decreases with increase in temperature due to which resistivity increases with increase in temperature. In semiconductors, the increase in number density (with increase in temperature) is more than the decrease in relaxation time, the net result is therefore a decrease in resistivity with increase in temperature.

question_html: Define relaxation time of elections in a conductor. Explain how it varies with increase in temperature of a conductor. State the relation between resistivity and relaxation time.
question_text: Define relaxation time of elections in a conductor. Explain how it varies with increase in temperature of a conductor. State the relation between resistivity and relaxation time.
explanation: Relaxation time is the time interval between two successive collisions of electrons in a conductor, when current flows. As the temperature increases, the thermal energy of electrons increases. Thus they collide more frequently with the atoms and hence the average relaxation time which is the average time between successive collisions decreases. Thus temperature is inversely proportional to relaxation time. Electrical resistivity is inversely proportional to relaxation time.

question_text: State the two rules that serve as general rules for analysis of electric circuit. Use these rules to write the three equations that may be used to obtain the values of the three unknown currents in the branches of the circuit given below:
explanation: Kirchoff's First law or Junction Rule: The algebraic sum of electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction i.e. Kirchhoff's Second Law or Voltage Rule: In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emf's of cell and the product of currents and resistance is always equal to zero i.e. Two rules of Kirchhoff are used for analysis of the electrical circuit In loop ABCA -I 2 R 1 - (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 - I 1 r + E 1 = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I 2 R 1 + (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 + I 1 r = E 1 ...(i) In loop ACDA I 1 r - (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 + (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 - E 1 = 0 I 1 r - (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 + (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 = E 1 ....(ii) In loop ABCDA -I 2 R 1 - (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 - (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 + (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 - E 1 = 0 I 2 R 1 + (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 + (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 - (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 = E 1 ....(iii)
solution: Kirchoff's First law or Junction Rule: The algebraic sum of electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction i.e. Kirchhoff's Second Law or Voltage Rule: In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emf's of cell and the product of currents and resistance is always equal to zero i.e. Two rules of Kirchhoff are used for analysis of the electrical circuit In loop ABCA -I 2 R 1 - (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 - I 1 r + E 1 = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} I 2 R 1 + (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 + I 1 r = E 1 ...(i) In loop ACDA I 1 r - (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 + (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 - E 1 = 0 I 1 r - (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 + (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 = E 1 ....(ii) In loop ABCDA -I 2 R 1 - (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 - (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 + (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 - E 1 = 0 I 2 R 1 + (I 1 + I 2 )R 3 + (I 2 + I 3 - I 1 )R 4 - (I 1 - I 2 )R 2 = E 1 ....(iii)
solution_html:
Kirchoff's First law or Junction Rule: The algebraic sum of electric currents at any junction of electric circuit is equal to zero i.e. the sum of current entering into a junction is equal to the sum of current leaving the junction i.e.
Kirchhoff's Second Law or Voltage Rule: In any closed mesh of electrical circuit, the algebraic sum of emf's of cell and the product of currents and resistance is always equal to zero i.e.

Two rules of Kirchhoff are used for analysis of the electrical circuit

In loop ABCA
-I2R1 - (I1 + I2)R3 - I1r + E1 = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} I2R1 + (I1 + I2)R3 + I1r = E1 ...(i)
In loop ACDA
I1r - (I2 + I3 - I1)R4 + (I1 - I2)R2 - E1 = 0
I1r - (I2 + I3 - I1)R4 + (I1 - I2)R2 = E1 ....(ii)
In loop ABCDA
-I2R1 - (I1 + I2)R3 - (I2 + I3 - I1)R4 + (I1 - I2)R2 - E1 = 0
I2R1 + (I1 + I2)R3 + (I2 + I3 - I1)R4 - (I1 - I2)R2 = E1 ....(iii)

question_html:
1. Differentiate between electrical resistance and resistivity of a conductor.
  2. Two metallic rods, each of length L, area of cross A1 and A2, having resistivities {tex}\rho_1{/tex} and {tex}\rho_2{/tex} are connected in parallel across a d.c. battery. Obtain the expression for the effective resistivity of this combination.
question_text: Differentiate between electrical resistance and resistivity of a conductor. Two metallic rods, each of length L, area of cross A 1 and A 2, having resistivities {tex}\rho_1{/tex} and {tex}\rho_2{/tex} are connected in parallel across a d.c. battery. Obtain the expression for the effective resistivity of this combination.
explanation: Electrical resistance Resistivity It is the property of material due to which it opposes the flow of electricity through the conductor. The resistivity is defined as the resistance of a material of 1 metre length and 1 square metre area of cross section. Unit : Ohm Unit : Ohm-metre Symbol : R Symbol : {tex}\rho{/tex} Depends on length, cross-section area of conductor and temperature. Depends on temperature and material of the conductor. For resistor R 1 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_1{/tex} Length = L Area = A 1 {tex}\therefore R_1=\rho_1 \times \frac{L}{A_1}{/tex} For resistor R 2 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_2{/tex} Length = L Area = A 2 {tex}\therefore R_2=\rho_2 \times \frac{L}{A_2}{/tex} For the equivalent resistor, Resistivity = {tex}\rho{/tex} Length = L Area = A = A 1 + A 2 {tex}\therefore R=\rho \times \frac{L}{A}{/tex} Since, the resistors are connected in parallel, Equivalent resistance {tex}=R=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}{/tex} or, {tex}\rho \times \frac{L}{A}=\frac{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1} \times \frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1}+\frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = Effective resistivity {tex}=\frac{\rho_1 \rho_2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{\rho_1 A_2+\rho_2 A_1}{/tex}
solution: Electrical resistance Resistivity It is the property of material due to which it opposes the flow of electricity through the conductor. The resistivity is defined as the resistance of a material of 1 metre length and 1 square metre area of cross section. Unit : Ohm Unit : Ohm-metre Symbol : R Symbol : {tex}\rho{/tex} Depends on length, cross-section area of conductor and temperature. Depends on temperature and material of the conductor. For resistor R 1 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_1{/tex} Length = L Area = A 1 {tex}\therefore R_1=\rho_1 \times \frac{L}{A_1}{/tex} For resistor R 2 , Resistivity = {tex}\rho_2{/tex} Length = L Area = A 2 {tex}\therefore R_2=\rho_2 \times \frac{L}{A_2}{/tex} For the equivalent resistor, Resistivity = {tex}\rho{/tex} Length = L Area = A = A 1 + A 2 {tex}\therefore R=\rho \times \frac{L}{A}{/tex} Since, the resistors are connected in parallel, Equivalent resistance {tex}=R=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}{/tex} or, {tex}\rho \times \frac{L}{A}=\frac{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1} \times \frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1}+\frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = Effective resistivity {tex}=\frac{\rho_1 \rho_2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{\rho_1 A_2+\rho_2 A_1}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. Electrical resistance Resistivity
    It is the property of material due to which it opposes the flow of electricity through the conductor. The resistivity is defined as the resistance of a material of 1 metre length and 1 square metre area of cross section.
    Unit : Ohm Unit : Ohm-metre
    Symbol : R Symbol : {tex}\rho{/tex}
    Depends on length, cross-section area of conductor and temperature. Depends on temperature and material of the conductor.
  2. For resistor R1,
    Resistivity = {tex}\rho_1{/tex}
    Length = L
    Area = A1
    {tex}\therefore R_1=\rho_1 \times \frac{L}{A_1}{/tex}
    For resistor R2,
    Resistivity = {tex}\rho_2{/tex}
    Length = L
    Area = A2
    {tex}\therefore R_2=\rho_2 \times \frac{L}{A_2}{/tex}
    For the equivalent resistor,
    Resistivity = {tex}\rho{/tex}
    Length = L
    Area = A = A1 + A2
    {tex}\therefore R=\rho \times \frac{L}{A}{/tex}
    Since, the resistors are connected in parallel,
    Equivalent resistance {tex}=R=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}{/tex}
    or, {tex}\rho \times \frac{L}{A}=\frac{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1} \times \frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{\frac{\rho_1 L}{A_1}+\frac{\rho_2 L}{A_2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\rho{/tex} = Effective resistivity {tex}=\frac{\rho_1 \rho_2\left(A_1+A_2\right)}{\rho_1 A_2+\rho_2 A_1}{/tex}

question_text: Two cells of emf 2E and E and internal resistances 2r and r respectively, are connected in parallel. Obtain the expressions for the equivalent emf and the internal resistance of the combination.
explanation: Given, emf of first cell = 2E emf of second cell = E Internal resistance of first cell = 2r Internal resistance of second cell = r Net current, I = I 1 + I 2 ...(i) E 1 = 2E For cell-I V = V A - V B = 2E - I 1 (2r) {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r }{/tex} ....(ii) For cell-II, V = V A - V B = E - I 2 r {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}\because{/tex} From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), substituting in Eq. (i), we get {tex}I = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r } + \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} On rearranging the term, we get {tex}V = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } - I \left( \frac { 2 r } { 3 } \right){/tex} But for equivalent of combination, V = E eq - I(r eq ) On comparing, {tex}E _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } , r _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 2 r } { 3 }{/tex}

(ii) and (iii), substituting in Eq. solution_html:
Given, emf of first cell = 2E
emf of second cell = E
Internal resistance of first cell = 2r
Internal resistance of second cell = r
Net current, I = I1 + I2 ...(i)
E1 = 2E

For cell-I
V = VA - VB = 2E - I1(2r)
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 1 } = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r }{/tex} ....(ii)
For cell-II, V = VA - VB = E - I2r
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad I _ { 2 } = \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex} ...(iii)
{tex}\because{/tex} From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), substituting in Eq. (i), we get
{tex}I = \frac { 2 E - V } { 2 r } + \frac { E - V } { r }{/tex}
On rearranging the term, we get
{tex}V = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } - I \left( \frac { 2 r } { 3 } \right){/tex}

V = Eeq - I(req)
On comparing,
{tex}E _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 4 E } { 3 } , r _ { \mathrm { eq } } = \frac { 2 r } { 3 }{/tex}

question_html: Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in Fig.
question_text: Determine the current in each branch of the network shown in Fig.
explanation: Each branch of the network is assigned an unknown current to be determined by the application of Kirchhoff’s rules. To reduce the number of unknowns at the outset, the first rule of Kirchhoff is used at every junction to assign the unknown current in each branch. (current entering into the junction is equal to current leaving the junction) We then have three unknowns I 1 , I 2 and I 3 which can be found by applying the second rule of Kirchhoff to three different closed loops. Kirchhoff’s second rule for the closed-loop ADCA gives, 10 – 4(I 1 – I 2 ) + 2(I 2 + I 3 – I 1 ) – I 1 = 0 ...(i) that is, 7I 1 – 6I 2 – 2I 3 = 10 For the closed-loop ABCA, we get 10 – 4I 2 – 2 (I 2 + I 3 ) – I 1 = 0 that is, I 1 + 6I 2 + 2I 3 = 10 ...(ii) For the closed-loop BCDEB, we get 5 – 2 (I 2 + I 3 ) – 2 (I 2 + I 3 – I 1 ) = 0 that is, 2I 1 – 4I 2 – 4I 3 = –5 ...(iii) Equations (i, ii, iii) are three simultaneous equations in three unknowns. These can be solved by the usual method to give I 1 = 2.5A, I 2 = {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} A, I 3 = 1 {tex}\frac{7}{8}{/tex} A The currents in the various branches of the network are AB : {tex}\frac{5}{8}{/tex} A, CA : {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} A, DEB : {tex}1 \frac{7}{8}{/tex} A AD : {tex}1 \frac{7}{8}{/tex} A, CD : 0 A, BC : {tex}2 \frac{1}{2}{/tex} A It is easily verified that Kirchhoff’s second rule applied to the remaining closed loops does not provide any additional independent equation, that is, the above values of currents satisfy the second rule for every closed loop of the network. For example, the total voltage drop over the closed-loop BADEB {tex}5 \mathrm{V}+\left(\frac{5}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}-\left(\frac{15}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}{/tex} equal to zero, as required by Kirchhoff’s second rule.

{tex}5 \mathrm{V}+\left(\frac{5}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}-\left(\frac{15}{8} \times 4\right) \mathrm{V}{/tex} equal to zero, as required by Kirchhoff’s second rule.

question_html: Use Kirchhoff's rules to obtain conditions for the balance conditions in a Wheatstone bridge.
question_text: Use Kirchhoff's rules to obtain conditions for the balance conditions in a Wheatstone bridge.
explanation: Applying Kirchhoff's loop law to close loop ABDA, we get I 1 R - I g G - I 2 P = 0 ...(i) Here, G is the resistance of the galvanometer. Applying Kirchhoff's loop law in the closed loop BDCB,we get {tex}I _ { g } G + \left( I _ { 1 } + I _ { g } \right) S - \left( I _ { 2 } - I _ { g } \right) Q = 0{/tex} .. (ii) When the Wheatstone bridge is balanced, no current flows through the galvanometer, i.e. I g = 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} From Eq. (i), we get I 1 R - I 2 P = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 R = I 2 P {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \frac { I _ { 1 } } { I _ { 2 } } = \frac { P } { R }{/tex} .. (iii) Similarly,from Eq. (ii), we get I 1 S - I 2 Q = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I 1 S = I 2 Q = 0..... (iv) From Eqs. (iii) and (iv), we get {tex}\frac { P } { R } = \frac { Q } { S } \Rightarrow \frac { P } { Q } = \frac { R } { S }{/tex} This is the required balance condition in a Wheatstone bridge arrangement and also helps us to determine unknown resistance.

question_text: Two cells of emf E 1 , E 2 and internal resistances r 1 and r 2 respectively are connected in parallel as shown in the figure. Deduce the expressions for the equivalent emf of the combination. the equivalent resistance of the combination.
explanation: Let us assume that I 1 = current through E 1 and I 2 = current through {tex}E_2{/tex} according to Kirchhoffs first rule, I = I 1 + I 2 Now, let V = potential difference across the parallel combination . Then {tex}V = E _ { 1 } - I _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } \text { or } I _ { 1 } = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex} , and similarly {tex}I _ { 2 } = \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} Thus, substituting for I 1 and I 2 , we have {tex}I = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } - V } { r _ { 2 } }{/tex} or {tex}I = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { E _ { 2 } } { r _ { 2 } } \right) - V \left( \frac { 1 } { r _ { 1 } } + \frac { 1 } { r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} {tex}V = \left( \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right) - I \left( \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } } \right){/tex} ...(i) Comparing the above equation with, V = E eq - Ir eq we get {tex}E_{eq} = \frac { E _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } + E _ { 2 } r _ { 1 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex} This is equivalent emf of the combination. {tex}r_{eq}= \frac { r _ { 1 } r _ { 2 } } { r _ { 1 } + r _ { 2 } }{/tex} This is equivalent resistance of the combination

Let us assume that  I1 = current through E1 and I2 = current through {tex}E_2{/tex} 
according to Kirchhoffs first rule,  I = I1 + I2
Now, let V = potential difference across the parallel combination . Then 
{tex}V = E _ { 1 } - I _ { 1 } r _ { 1 } \text { or } I _ { 1 } = \frac { E _ { 1 } - V } { r _ { 1 } }{/tex}, and similarly

question_html: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons in a conductor in terms of relaxation time.
question_text: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons in a conductor in terms of relaxation time.
explanation: The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity. When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force F = - eE, and free electron acquires an acceleration, a= F/m = - eE/m ... (i) where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons in a conductor in a presence of voltage source.

The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. The drift velocity is the average velocity that a particle, such as an electron, attains in a material due to an electric field. It can also be referred to as axial drift velocity.

When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force
F = - eE, and free electron acquires an
acceleration, a= F/m = - eE/m ... (i)
where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field.

{tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii)
where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} v1, v2, ... vn, are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u1, u2, ..., un, respectively.
{tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex}
Similarly,
{tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex}
{tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex}
The drift speed vd may be defined as
{tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex}
{tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
{tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
{tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0]

question_text: Define the term of drift velocity. On the basis of electron drift, derive an expression for resistivity of a conductor in terms of number density of free electrons and relaxation time. On what factors does resistivity of a conductor depend? Why alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors?
explanation: Drift Velocity : It the average velocity with which electrons move along the conductor under influence of electric field. Or Average velocity acquired by the electrons in the conductor in the presence of external electric field. {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { I } { n e A }{/tex} Specific resistance or resistivity of the material of a conductor is defined as the resistance of a unit length with unit area of cross-section of the material of the conductor. The unit of resistivity is ohm-meter or {tex}\Omega{/tex} -m. we know that {tex}R = \rho ( l / A ){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \rho = R A / l{/tex} From Ohm's law, we get V = IR {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} E {tex}l{/tex} = neAv d R {tex}\Rightarrow \quad R = E l / n e A v _ { d } \text { and } v _ { d } = e E \tau / m{/tex} So, {tex}R = \frac { E l \times m } { n e ^ { 2 } A E \tau } = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} Substituting the value of {tex}R = \frac { m l } { n e ^ { 2 } A \tau }{/tex} in Eq. (i), we have {tex}\rho = \left( m l / n e ^ { 2 } A \tau \right) \cdot A / l{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Resistivity of the material is given by, {tex}\rho = m / n e ^ { 2 } \tau{/tex} where {tex}\tau = {/tex} average relaxation time. From the above formula, it is clear that resistivity of a conductor depends upon the following factors: {tex}\rho \propto \frac { 1 } { n }{/tex} , i.e., the resistivity of material is inversely proportional to the number density of free electrons . As the free electron density depends upon the nature of material, so resistivity of a conductor depends on the nature of the material. {tex}\rho \propto \mathbf { 1 } / \tau{/tex} , i.e. the resistivity of a material is inversely proportional to the average relaxation time {tex}\tau{/tex} of free electrons in the conductor. As the value of {tex}\tau{/tex} depends on the temperature as temperature increases, {tex}\tau{/tex} decreases, hence {tex}\rho{/tex} increases. Alloys like Constantan and Manganin are used for making standard resistors because they have high value of resistivity temperature coefficient of resistance is less

1. Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10–7 m2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. Assume that each copper atom contributes roughly one conduction electron. The density of copper is 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 103 kg/m3, and its atomic mass is 63.5 u.
  2. Compare the drift speed obtained above with,
    1. thermal speeds of copper atoms at ordinary temperatures,
    2. speed of propagation of electric field along the conductor which causes the drift motion.
question_text: Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –7 m 2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. Assume that each copper atom contributes roughly one conduction electron. The density of copper is 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 3 kg/m 3 , and its atomic mass is 63.5 u. Compare the drift speed obtained above with, thermal speeds of copper atoms at ordinary temperatures, speed of propagation of electric field along the conductor which causes the drift motion.
explanation: The direction of drift velocity of conduction electrons is opposite to the electric field direction, i.e., electrons drift in the direction of increasing potential. The drift speed v d is given by Equation ,v d = (I/neA) Now, e = 1.6 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –19 C, A = 1.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –7 m 2 , I = 1.5 A. The density of n conduction electrons, is equal to the number of atoms per cubic metre (assuming one conduction electron per Cu atom as is reasonable from its valence electron count of one). A cubic metre of copper has a mass of 9.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 3 kg. Since 6.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 23 copper atoms have a mass of 63.5 g, {tex}n=\frac{6.0 \times 10^{23}}{63.5} \times 9.0 \times 10^{6}{/tex} number of the conduction electron per unit volume is ,n= 8.5 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 28 m –3 which gives, {tex}v_{d}=\frac{1.5}{8.5 \times 10^{28} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{/tex} = 1.1 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 –3 m s –1 = 1.1 mm s –1 At a temperature T, the thermal speed* of a copper atom of mass M is obtained from <( {tex}\frac 12{/tex} ) Mv 2 > = (3/2) k B T ] and is thus typical of the order of {tex}\sqrt{k_{B} T / M}{/tex} (relation between velocity and temperature can be find by this relation) , where k B is the Boltzmann constant. For copper at 300 K, this is about 2 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 2 m/s. There will be the random vibrational speeds of copper atoms in a conductor. Note that the drift speed of electrons is much smaller, about 10 –5 times the typical thermal speed at ordinary temperatures. An electric field travelling along the conductor has a speed of an electromagnetic wave, namely equal to 3.0 {tex}\times {/tex} 10 8 m s –1 . The drift speed is, in comparison, extremely small; smaller by a factor of 10 –11 .(speed must be less than speed of light)

question_html:
1. You are required to select a carbon resistor of resistance of {tex}470 \Omega \pm {/tex} 5% from a shopkeeper. What would be the sequence of colour bands required to code the desired resistor?
  2. Explain briefly why electric power from power stations to homes/factories is delivered via transmission cables at high voltages.
question_text: You are required to select a carbon resistor of resistance of {tex}470 \Omega \pm {/tex} 5% from a shopkeeper. What would be the sequence of colour bands required to code the desired resistor? Explain briefly why electric power from power stations to homes/factories is delivered via transmission cables at high voltages.
explanation: {tex}470 \Omega \pm {/tex} 5% 4 stands for yellow colour. 7 stands for violet colour. 0 implies 10 1 and it stands for Brown colour. 5% implies gold colour. Imagine that a device of resistance R, needs a power P for its working. If V is the voltages across R and I is the current through it, we have P = VI i.e. {tex}I=\frac{P}{V}{/tex} Let the transmission cables have a resistance Rc, the power, dissipated in the connecting wires (say P c ) is then given by {tex}P_c=I^2 R_c{/tex} {tex}=\frac{P^2 R_c}{V^2}{/tex} This power gets wasted as heat during transmission. We see that, to operate a device of power P, the power, wasted in the connecting wires is inversely proportional to V 2 , therefore at high voltage, less power well get wasted in the transmission cables. It follows that by transmitting power from power stations to homes/factories, via transmission cables, at high voltages, we can bring about a very significant reduction in the power wasted during transmission.

question_html:
Read the following paragraph and answer the questions that follow. (4 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 = 4)
In a metallic conductor, an electron, moving due to thermal motion, suffers collisions with the heavy fixed ions but after collision, it will emerge out with the same speed but in random directions. If we consider all the electrons, their average velocity will be zero. When an electric field is applied, electrons move with an average velocity, known as drift velocity ({tex}{v}_{{d}}{/tex}). The average time between successive collisions is known as relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex}. The magnitude of drift velocity per unit electric field is called mobility ({tex}\mu{/tex}).
An expression for current through the conductor can be obtained in terms of drift velocity, number of electrons per unit volume (n), electronic charge (-e), and the cross-sectional area of the conductor. This expression leads to an expression between current density {tex}(\hat{j}){/tex} and the electric field ({tex}\vec{E}{/tex}). Hence, an expression for resistivity ({tex}\rho{/tex}) of a metal is obtained. This expression helps us to understand increase in resistivity of a metal with increase in its temperature, in terms of change in the relaxation time {tex}(\tau){/tex} and change in the number density of electrons {tex}({n}){/tex}.

  1. Consider two cylindrical conductors A and B, made of the same metal connected in series to a battery. The length and the radius of B are twice that of A. If {tex}\mu_{{A}}{/tex} and {tex}\mu_{{B}}{/tex} are the mobility of electrons in A and {tex}B{/tex} respectively, then {tex}\frac{\mu_A}{\mu_B}{/tex} is:
    1. {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
    2. {tex}\frac{1}{4}{/tex}
    3. 2
    4. 1
  2. A wire of length 0.5 m and cross-sectional area {tex}1.0 \times 10^{-7} {~m}^2{/tex} is connected to a battery of 2 V that maintains a current of 1.5 A in it. The conductivity of the material of the wire (in {tex}\Omega^{-1} {~m}^{-1}{/tex}) is:
    1. {tex}2.5 \times 10^4{/tex}
    2. {tex}3.0 \times 10^5{/tex}
    3. {tex}3.75 \times 10^6{/tex}
    4. {tex}5.0 \times 10^7{/tex}
  3. The temperature coefficient of resistance of nichrome is {tex}1.70 \times 10^{-4}{ }^{\circ} {C}^{-1}{/tex}. In order to increase resistance of a nichrome wire by {tex}8.5 \%{/tex}, the temperature of the wire should be increased by:
    1. {tex}250^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    2. {tex}500^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    3. {tex}850^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    4. {tex}1000^{\circ} {C}{/tex}
    1. Consider the contribution of the following two factors I and II in resistivity of a metal:
      1. Relaxation time of electrons
      2. Number of electrons per unit volume
        The resistivity of a metal increases with increase in its temperature because:
        1. I decreases and II increases.
        2. I increases and II is almost constant.
        3. Both I and II increase.
        4. I decreases and II is almost constant.

          OR

    2. A steady current flows in a copper wire of non-uniform cross-section. Consider the following three physical quantities:
      1. Electric field
      2. Current density
      3. Drift speed
        Then at the different points along the wire:
        1. II and III change, but I is constant.
        2. I and II change, but III is constant.
        3. I and III change, but II is constant.
        4. All I, II and III change.

question_html:
1. State the two Kirchhoff's rules used in the analysis of electric circuits and explain them.
  2. Derive the equation of the balanced state in a Wheatstone bridge using Kirchhoff's laws.
question_text: State the two Kirchhoff's rules used in the analysis of electric circuits and explain them. Derive the equation of the balanced state in a Wheatstone bridge using Kirchhoff's laws.
explanation: Junction rule: At any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction. Loop rule: The algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero. Using Kirchoff's seconds law to the loop ABDA, we get I 1 P - I g G - I 2 R = 0: G is the galvanometer resistance. Applying Kirchoff's law to the loop ABDA, we get (I 1 - I g )Q - (I 2 - I g )S - GI g = 0 When the bridge is balanced I g = 0 Then, the equations can be written as, I 1 P - I 2 R = 0 or I 1 P = I 2 R ...(1) I 1 Q - I 2 S = 0 or I 1 Q = I 2 S ...(2) On dividing equation (1) by (2), we get {tex}\frac {\text P}{\text Q}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\text R}{\text S}{/tex} , which is the balanced condition of a Wheatstone bridge.

Using Kirchoff's seconds law to the loop ABDA, we get I 1 P - I g G - I 2 R = 0: G is the galvanometer resistance. Applying Kirchoff's law to the loop ABDA, we get (I 1 - I g )Q - (I 2 - I g )S - GI g = 0 When the bridge is balanced I g = 0 Then, the equations can be written as, I 1 P - I 2 R = 0 or I 1 P = I 2 R ...(1) I 1 Q - I 2 S = 0 or I 1 Q = I 2 S ...(2) On dividing equation (1) by (2), we get {tex}\frac {\text P}{\text Q}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\text R}{\text S}{/tex} , which is the balanced condition of a Wheatstone bridge.

1. Junction rule: At any junction, the sum of the currents entering the junction is equal to the sum of currents leaving the junction.
    Loop rule: The algebraic sum of changes in potential around any closed loop involving resistors and cells in the loop is zero.

  2. Using Kirchoff's seconds law to the loop ABDA, we get I1P - IgG - I2R = 0: G is the galvanometer resistance. Applying Kirchoff's law to the loop ABDA, we get (I1 - Ig)Q - (I2- Ig)S - GIg = 0 When the bridge is balanced Ig = 0 Then, the equations can be written as, I1 P - I2R = 0 or I1P = I2R ...(1) I1Q - I2S = 0 or I1Q = I2S ...(2) On dividing equation (1) by (2), we get {tex}\frac {\text P}{\text Q}{/tex} = {tex}\frac {\text R}{\text S}{/tex}, which is the balanced condition of a Wheatstone bridge.

explanation: Let I 1 and I 2 be the currents in two cells with emfs, E 1 and E 2 and internal resistances, r 1 and r 2 , So, I = I 1 + I 2 No, let v be the potential difference between the points, A and B. Since, the first cell is connected between the points A and B. V = Potential difference across first cell V = E 1 - I 1 r 1 or I 1 = {tex}\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}{/tex} Now, the second cell is also connected between the points, A and B. So, {tex}I_2=\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} Thus, substituting for I 1 and I 2 , {tex}I=\frac{E_1-V}{r_1}+\frac{E_2-V}{r_2}{/tex} or {tex}I=\left(\frac{E_1}{r_1}+\frac{E_2}{r_2}\right)-V\left(\frac{1}{r_1}+\frac{1}{r_2}\right){/tex} {tex}V=\left(\frac{E_1 r_2+E_2 r_1}{r_1+r_2}\right)-I\left(\frac{r_1 r_2}{r_1+r_2}\right){/tex} ...(i) If E is effective emf and r, the effective internal resistance of the parallel combination of the two cells, then V = E - Ir ...(ii) Comparing Eqs.   2. How does drift velocity of electrons in a metallic conductor vary with increase in temperature? Explain.
question_text: Derive an expression for drift velocity of free electrons. How does drift velocity of electrons in a metallic conductor vary with increase in temperature? Explain.
explanation: When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force: F = - eE, and free electron acquires an acceleration, a = F/m = -eE/m ... (i) where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and can be calculated as - {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii) where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference(relaxation time) between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions. {tex}\therefore{/tex} v 1 , v 2 , ... v n , are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u 1 , u 2 , ..., u n , respectively. {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex} Similarly, {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex} {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex} The drift speed v d may be defined as {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex} {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [ {tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0] {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)] This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons which shows it is directly proportional to relaxation time. As the temperature of a conductor is increased, the thermal agitation increases and the collisions become more frequent. The average time {tex}\tau{/tex} between the successive collisions decreases and hence the drift speed decreases. Thus, the conductivity decreases and the resistivity of the conductor increases.

solution_html:
1. When a conductor is subjected to an electric field E, each electron experiences a force:
    F = - eE, and free electron acquires an
    acceleration, a = F/m = -eE/m ... (i)
    where, m = mass of electron, e = electronic charge and E = electric field.
    Free electron starts accelerating and gains velocity and collide with atoms and molecules of the conductor. The average time difference between two consecutive collisions is known as relaxation time of electron and can be calculated as -
    {tex}\overline { \tau } = \frac { \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } } { n }{/tex} ...(ii)
    where, {tex}\tau_{1}, \; \tau_{2}.... \; \tau_{n}{/tex} are the average time difference(relaxation time) between 1st, 2nd, ...nth collisions.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} v1, v2, ... vn, are velocities gained by electron in 1st, 2nd, ..., nth collisions with initial thermal velocities u1, u2, ..., un, respectively.
    {tex}\therefore \quad { v } _ { 1 } = { u } _ { 1 } + { a } \tau _ { 1 }{/tex}
    Similarly,
    {tex}\mathop {{v_2}}\limits_ \vdots = \mathop {{u_2}}\limits_ \vdots + \mathop {a{\tau _2}}\limits_ \vdots {/tex}
    {tex}{v_n} = {u_n} + a{\tau _2}{/tex}
    The drift speed vd may be defined as
    {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { v _ { 1 } + v _ { 2 } + \ldots + v _ { n } } { n }{/tex}
    {tex}v _ { d } = \frac { \left( u _ { 1 } + u _ { 2 } + \ldots + u _ { n } \right) + a \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
    {tex} { v } _ { d } = \frac { \left( { u } _ { 1 } + { u } _ { 2 } + \ldots + { u } _ { n } \right) } { n } + \frac { { a } \left( \tau _ { 1 } + \tau _ { 2 } + \ldots + \tau _ { n } \right) } { n }{/tex}
    {tex}v _ { d } = 0 + { a } \tau{/tex} [{tex}\because{/tex} Average thermal velocity in n collisions = 0]
    {tex}{ v } _ { d } = - ( e { E } / m ) \tau{/tex} [from Eq. (i)]
    This is the required expression of drift speed of free electrons which shows it is directly proportional to relaxation time.

  2. As the temperature of a conductor is increased, the thermal agitation increases and the collisions become more frequent. The average time {tex}\tau{/tex} between the successive collisions decreases and hence the drift speed decreases. Thus, the conductivity decreases and the resistivity of the conductor increases.

1. Derive the relation between current density J and potential difference V across a current carrying conductor of length l, area of cross-section A and the number density n of free electrons.
  2. Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10-7 m2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. [Assume that the number density of conduction electrons is 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 1028 m-3]

Estimate the average drift speed of conduction electrons in a copper wire of cross-sectional area 1.0 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 -7 m 2 carrying a current of 1.5 A. [Assume that the number density of conduction electrons is 9 {tex}\times{/tex} 10 28 m -3 ]
explanation: Let us consider a conductor of length ' l ' , cross-sectional Area ' A ' and having ' n ' number of electrons per unit volume drifting with drift velocity {tex}v_d{/tex} . {tex}I = neAv_d ...(i){/tex} When these electrons are moving, they collide with another electrons and in between the two successive collision the time taken is called relaxation time {tex}(\tau).{/tex} Electric field inside the wire is given by E = V/l ...(ii) where,E is electric field, l is the length of the conductor and V is potential. If relaxation time is {tex}\tau{/tex} , the drift speed {tex}(v_d){/tex} is given by, {tex}v _ { d } = e \tau E / m{/tex} where, m = mass of electron, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation charge, e = electronic charge and E = electric field. Putting the value of V d in Eq. (i). we get {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } { m } A E{/tex} ...(iii) {tex}I = m e ^ { 2 } \tau A V / m l{/tex} [From Eqs. (ii)] {tex}\Rightarrow J = I / A = n e ^ { 2 } \tau V / m l{/tex} Given, {tex}I = 1.5 A , n = 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 }{/tex} {tex}A = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex} {tex}\because \quad v _ { d } ={ I \over { neA }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = \frac { 15 } { 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \times 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = 1.04 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}

1. Let us consider a conductor of length ' l ' , cross-sectional Area ' A ' and having ' n ' number of electrons per unit volume drifting with drift velocity {tex}v_d{/tex}.
    {tex}I = neAv_d ...(i){/tex}
    When these electrons are moving, they collide with another electrons and in between the two successive collision the time taken is called relaxation time {tex}(\tau).{/tex}
    Electric field inside the wire is given by
    E = V/l ...(ii)
    where,E is electric field, l is the length of the conductor and V is potential.
    If relaxation time is {tex}\tau{/tex}, the drift speed {tex}(v_d){/tex} is given by,
    {tex}v _ { d } = e \tau E / m{/tex}
    where, m = mass of electron, {tex}\tau{/tex} = relaxation charge, e = electronic charge and E = electric field.
    Putting the value of Vd in Eq. (i). we get
    {tex}\Rightarrow \quad I = \frac { n e ^ { 2 } \tau } { m } A E{/tex} ...(iii)
    {tex}I = m e ^ { 2 } \tau A V / m l{/tex} [From Eqs. (ii)]
    {tex}\Rightarrow J = I / A = n e ^ { 2 } \tau V / m l{/tex}
  2. Given, {tex}I = 1.5 A , n = 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \mathrm { m } ^ { - 3 }{/tex}
    {tex}A = 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } \mathrm { m } ^ { 2 }{/tex}
    {tex}\because \quad v _ { d } ={ I \over { neA }}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = \frac { 15 } { 9 \times 10 ^ { 28 } \times 1.6 \times 10 ^ { - 19 } \times 1.0 \times 10 ^ { - 7 } }{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow v _ { d } = 1.04 \times 10 ^ { - 3 } \mathrm { m } / \mathrm { s }{/tex}

question_html:
1. Define the term conductivity of a metallic wire. Write its SI unit.
  2. Using the concept of free electrons in a conductor, derive the expression for the conductivity of wire in terms of number density and relaxation time. Hence obtain the relation between current density and the applied electric field E.
question_text: Define the term conductivity of a metallic wire. Write its SI unit. Using the concept of free electrons in a conductor, derive the expression for the conductivity of wire in terms of number density and relaxation time. Hence obtain the relation between current density and the applied electric field E.
explanation: The property of a material that allows flows of electrons (or any charged particle) between two points of the material when a potential difference is applied between these two points is called conductivity of a wire. SI unit of conductivity: Siemens per metre Drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is given as {tex}v_{d}=\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex} ....... (1) where, E = electric field set up across a conductor m = mass of electron {tex}\tau{/tex} = average relaxation time Now the current flowing the conductor can be derived as We know that l = neAv d ..... (2) n {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Number of free electrons per unit volume or number density Now from equation (1) and (2), we get {tex}I=\frac{n e^{2} A \tau E}{m}{/tex} ...... (3) Since resistivity of a conductor is given as {tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex} Now, we know that conductivity of a conductor is mathematically defined as the reciprocal of resistivity of the conductor. Thus, {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\sigma}{/tex} ....... (4) where, {tex}\sigma {/tex} = conductivity of the conductor. Thus, from equation (3) and (4), we get {tex}\sigma=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}{/tex} ....... (5) Now, from equation (3) and (5), we have {tex}\frac{I}{A}=\sigma E{/tex} ....... (6) and current density is given as J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} Thus, J = {tex}\sigma {/tex} E

Thus, {tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\sigma}{/tex} ....... (4) where, {tex}\sigma {/tex} = conductivity of the conductor. Thus, from equation (3) and (4), we get {tex}\sigma=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}{/tex} ....... (5) Now, from equation (3) and (5), we have {tex}\frac{I}{A}=\sigma E{/tex} ....... (6) and current density is given as J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex} Thus, J = {tex}\sigma {/tex} E
solution_html:
The property of a material that allows flows of electrons (or any charged particle) between two points of the material when a potential difference is applied between these two points is called conductivity of a wire.
SI unit of conductivity: Siemens per metre


Drift velocity of electrons in a conductor is given as
{tex}v_{d}=\frac{e E \tau}{m}{/tex} ....... (1)
where,
E = electric field set up across a conductor
m = mass of electron
{tex}\tau{/tex} = average relaxation time
Now the current flowing the conductor can be derived as

We know that
l = neAvd ..... (2)
n {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Number of free electrons per unit volume or number density
Now from equation (1) and (2), we get
{tex}I=\frac{n e^{2} A \tau E}{m}{/tex} ...... (3)
Since resistivity of a conductor is given as
{tex}\rho=\frac{m}{n e^{2} \tau}{/tex}
Now, we know that conductivity of a conductor is mathematically defined as the reciprocal of resistivity of the conductor. Thus,
{tex}\rho=\frac{1}{\sigma}{/tex} ....... (4)
where, {tex}\sigma {/tex} = conductivity of the conductor. Thus, from equation (3) and (4), we get
{tex}\sigma=\frac{n e^{2} \tau}{m}{/tex} ....... (5)
Now, from equation (3) and (5), we have
{tex}\frac{I}{A}=\sigma E{/tex} ....... (6)
and current density is given as
J = {tex}\frac{I}{A}{/tex}
Thus, J = {tex}\sigma {/tex}E

  2. Figure shows a plot of current I flowing through the cross-section of a wire versus the time t. Use the plot to find the charge flowing in 10 through the wire.
question_text: Deduce the relation between current I flowing through a conductor and drift velocity v d of the electrons. Figure shows a plot of current I flowing through the cross-section of a wire versus the time t. Use the plot to find the charge flowing in 10 through the wire.
explanation: The average velocity with which an electron moves through a conductor when potential difference is applied across it, is called drift velocity. let n = number density of electrons in the conductor. Then total charge per unit volume is Q = ne Total number of free electrons in the conductor N = n {tex}\times{/tex} volume of conductor PQ or N = nAl , l is length of the conductor and A is it's cross-sectional Area Time t in which an electron moves from P to Q, all N free electrons pass through cross-section Q. t = l/v d where, v d is the drift velocity of electrons in the conductor. So, electric current flowing through conductor is given by {tex}I = \frac { q } { t } = \frac { N e } { t } = \frac { n A l e } { l / v _ { d } } \Rightarrow I = n e A v _ { d }{/tex} This gives the relation between electric current and drift velocity. Area under I-t curve on t-axis is charge flowing through the conductor {tex}Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \times 5 \times 5 + ( 5 \times 5 )= 37.5 \mathrm { C }{/tex}
